Press enter or click to view image in full size

5 min read
Just now

--

How I built an OCR pipeline that processes 50K+ beverage distributor invoices daily using Qwen2.5-VL, Llama 3.2 Vision, and a human-in-the-loop feedback system.

Every beverage store receives a stack of invoices each week from half a dozen different distributors. Each distributor has its own layout, its own abbreviations, its own way of writing a case of wine. Someone has to type all of that into a database, line by line.
That “someone” used to be a human. This post is about how I replaced that manual entry with a fine-tuned vision-language model pipeline that now extracts structured data from 50K+ documents daily at 96% accuracy — and the engineering decisions, failures, and fixes along the way.
Why traditional OCR wasn’t enough
The obvious first attempt is classic OCR (Tesseract, AWS Textract) plus regex or template parsing. It breaks down fast:
Invoices are not one document type — they are N document types, where N is the number of distributors. Line items wrap unpredictably, discounts appear as negative rows or footnotes, and the same product might be “CH LAFITE ROTH PAUILLAC 750” on one invoice and “Chateau Lafite Rothschild 12/750ml” on another. Template-based parsing meant maintaining a fragile parser per distributor, and every layout tweak upstream silently broke extraction downstream.
Vision-language models change the shape of the problem. Instead of “read the characters, then figure out the structure,” a VLM does both at once: you hand it the invoice image and a target schema, and it returns structured JSON.
Architecture overview
The pipeline has five stages:

• Ingestion — invoices arrive as scans/PDFs and are queued for processing

• Extraction — a fine-tuned VLM (Qwen2.5-VL or Llama 3.2 Vision) converts each page into schema-validated JSON

• Validation — Pydantic v2 models reject malformed output before it touches the database

• Storage — normalized records land in PostgreSQL

• Feedback loop — low-confidence extractions route to human review, and corrections become training data
One schema to rule all distributors
The single most important design decision was defining a universal invoice schema before any fine-tuning. Every distributor’s document, no matter the layout, must map to the same two Pydantic models — one for line items, one for the invoice summary:
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from decimal import Decimalclass InvoiceLineItem(BaseModel):
product_name: str
product_code: str | None = None
quantity: int
unit: str # "case", "bottle", "keg"
unit_price: Decimal
extended_price: Decimal
discount: Decimal = Decimal("0")class InvoiceSummary(BaseModel):
distributor: str
invoice_number: str
invoice_date: str
subtotal: Decimal
tax: Decimal
total: Decimal
line_items: list[InvoiceLineItem]Why this matters: the schema becomes the contract between the model and the database. Model output that fails validation never reaches production — it fails loudly and routes to review. Pydantic v2’s strict typing catches the classic VLM sins: prices as strings, quantities hallucinated as floats, missing fields silently dropped.
Building the training data
I curated 200–500 labeled samples per distributor across 5+ invoice types. Two lessons here:
Quality beats quantity. Three hundred carefully verified samples per layout outperformed a first attempt with thousands of noisy auto-labeled ones. Every training label was validated against the Pydantic schema before it entered the dataset.
One shared format, distributor-specific prompts. All training data lives in a single JSONL format, but each sample’s prompt carries distributor context:
{
"messages": [
{"role": "user", "content": [
{"type": "image", "image": "invoice_page.jpg"},
{"type": "text", "text": "Extract this Distributor-A invoice. Note: this distributor uses '12/750' pack notation and lists depletion allowances as negative line items. Return JSON matching the UniversalInvoice schema."}
]},
{"role": "assistant", "content": "{\"distributor\": \"Distributor-A\", ...}"}
]
}This “prompt injection” strategy lets one fine-tuned model handle all distributors while still encoding layout-specific quirks — no per-distributor models to maintain.
Fine-tuning: Qwen2.5-VL and Llama 3.2 Vision
I fine-tuned two model families to compare, using Unsloth for LoRA training:

• DGX Spark (on-premise): full bf16 LoRA training

• AWS A10G (cloud): 4-bit QLoRA when GPU memory was the constraint
A representative Unsloth config:
from unsloth import FastVisionModelmodel, tokenizer = FastVisionModel.from_pretrained(
"unsloth/Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct",
load_in_4bit=True, # QLoRA on A10G; False for bf16 on DGX
)model = FastVisionModel.get_peft_model(
model,
r=16,
lora_alpha=16,
finetune_vision_layers=True, # invoice layouts are a visual task
finetune_language_layers=True,
)One non-obvious detail: finetune_vision_layers=True mattered a lot. Invoice extraction is as much about where things are on the page as what they say — tuning only the language layers plateaued well below the final accuracy.
For serving, models run on vLLM. On newer ARM64/Blackwell hardware I had to disable CUDA graph capture for compatibility — the kind of deployment detail that costs you a day and never appears in tutorials.
Surviving high volume: API resilience
Part of the pipeline uses Gemini 2.5 Flash for auxiliary extraction and product-name matching. At 50K+ documents a day, you hit rate limits constantly. The fix is boring and essential — rotate across multiple API keys and back off exponentially:
import itertools, timekey_pool = itertools.cycle(API_KEYS)def call_with_retry(prompt, max_retries=5):
for attempt in range(max_retries):
try:
return call_gemini(prompt, api_key=next(key_pool))
except RateLimitError:
time.sleep(2 ** attempt) # 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s...
raise ExtractionFailed(prompt)The human-in-the-loop feedback flywheel
The initial fine-tuned model launched at 89% accuracy. Good, but 11% of 50K documents is still 5,500 daily errors.
The fix wasn’t a bigger model — it was a feedback loop:

• Every extraction gets a confidence score (schema validation + field-level heuristics like “does the line-item sum match the subtotal?”)

• Low-confidence extractions route to a human review queue

• Corrections are stored as new training pairs — the original image plus the corrected JSON

• Periodic re-fine-tuning folds those corrections back into the model
Each cycle attacked the model’s actual production failure modes rather than generic weaknesses. Over successive iterations, accuracy climbed from 89% to 96%, and manual data entry dropped by 85%.
The compounding effect is the point: the errors humans fix today are precisely the errors the model stops making next month.
Results

• 96% extraction accuracy (from 89% at launch)

• 50K+ documents processed daily

• 85% reduction in manual data entry

• One model, 6+ distributor formats, zero per-distributor parsers to maintain
Key takeaways
Schema-first beats model-first. Defining strict Pydantic contracts before fine-tuning shaped everything: training data quality, validation, and safe failure modes.
Fine-tune the vision layers. For document tasks, layout is signal. Language-only LoRA left accuracy on the table.
A feedback loop outperforms a bigger model. The 89→96% gain came from targeted retraining on real production errors, not from scaling up.
Boring engineering carries the day. Key rotation, exponential backoff, confidence thresholds, human review queues — none of it is glamorous, and all of it is why the system survives at production volume.

I’m Roushan Singh, an AI/ML engineer working on LLM pipelines and vision-language systems. Find me on GitHub, LinkedIn, or check out my portfolio. Happy to answer questions in the comments.
